That's the trade, made with open eyes: speed surrendered in exactly one lane, to buy the guarantee that a whole class of mistake can't reach a user's phone.

Same developer, same day, three different amounts of ceremony — on purpose. And notice that almost none of it was decided in the moment. The reviewer subagent and the required check were built once, earlier, on a calm day, so that on a busy day the triage is half automatic. Hold that thought; it becomes a whole chapter later in the book.

Which brings us to the thing to actually do differently, and it costs one question. Before each task — before you type the prompt — ask: what happens if this is subtly wrong? Not wrong in the loud, crashing way that announces itself. Subtly wrong: plausible, running, incorrect. The size of that answer is the blast radius, and the blast radius decides the structure. A clumsy sentence on a docs page? Vibe freely; enjoy it. A silently corrupted library on a stranger's phone? Bring the checklist, the tests, and the gate.

Then take the second step, the one almost nobody takes: say your placement out loud, in the prompt itself — the first sentence you hand Claude Code. Literally type it. "This is a throwaway; skip ceremony." Or: "This ships; plan first, tests first." One sentence. It's the cheapest discipline available in this whole way of working, and it pays twice — once because the agent calibrates its effort to your stated stakes, and once because typing it forces you to have actually decided rather than drifted. Half the value lands on you, not the model. And if you find yourself typing the same placement for the same kind of task again and again, that's a rule that has outgrown the chat box — it belongs written down in CLAUDE.md, where it gets applied every session without you spending a thought on it.

The paper's own guidance says it almost exactly this way: the right position on the spectrum depends on the stakes. A weekend prototype can be pure vibe coding. A production API handling financial transactions demands agentic engineering. Most real work falls somewhere in between — and the skill is knowing where to draw the line for each task. That last clause is the job description. Not maximal rigor. Calibration, task by task.

One warning from experience: you'll be tempted to skip the question on small tasks, precisely because they're small. But small tasks are where the habit forms or doesn't. For a solo developer, there is no tech lead assigning risk levels — the culture around the tool is just your habits, repeated. Asking the blast-radius question every time, until it's reflex, is culture you are installing in yourself, one prompt at a time.

You now own this chapter's vocabulary: the spectrum, with vibe coding and agentic engineering as its endpoints and structured AI-assisted coding as the wide middle where most real work lives; six dimensions to place any task, from how intent is specified to the risk you're willing to carry; and, from Echo's own shelves, the docs folder at the calm end and the schema-migration-reviewer with its Build gate plus tests check at the careful end. Of those six dimensions, one towers over the rest — how the output gets verified — because it's the line between believing your code works and knowing it does. That one gets the next chapter to itself.
Chapter 5 — Proof Is the Differentiator

There was one line from the first edition of this book that listeners quoted back more than any other. It went like this: the green checkmark does not mean "this works." It means "this compiles." People wrote in about it. One reader said he'd stuck it on a note above his desk. Which is flattering, but also a little telling, because the line only names an instinct — that queasy feeling you get when everything looks fine and you haven't actually checked anything. It doesn't tell you what to do about it.

The paper does. Of all the sentences in the Google whitepaper this edition is built around, the one that does the most work is this one: the single biggest differentiator between vibe coding and agentic engineering is how outputs get verified. Not which model you use. Not how clever your prompts are. Not whether you have an agent at all. The whole spectrum from Chapter 4 — all six dimensions of it — hangs on this hinge. Everything else is a matter of degree; this is the thing that actually separates the two ends.

Here's the shape of it. In vibe coding, verification is optional. You ask for the code, the agent produces the code, you run it, and you check whether it seems right. "Seems right" is doing enormous work in that sentence. You clicked around, nothing exploded, the screen showed roughly what you expected — ship it. And to be fair, for a throwaway script or a weekend prototype, "seems right" is genuinely fine. Chapter 3 made that case and it still stands. But for anything you intend to keep, "seems right" is a feeling, and feelings don't catch the bug that only appears on the second launch, or the calculation that's off by one in a way the demo data happens to hide.

In agentic engineering, verification isn't a feeling. It's two mechanisms, working together, and the rest of this chapter is about what they are and how a solo developer at a kitchen table actually runs them.

Mechanism one is the one you've probably heard of: tests. A test verifies the deterministic parts of your system — the parts where, given the same input, the correct output is the same every single time. A function that takes a chapter's audio duration and a word count and returns a reading speed should return the same reading speed every time you hand it the same numbers. So you write a small piece of code that calls the function with known inputs and checks the output against the known right answer. Pass or fail. No opinion involved, no judgment call, no "seems right." The computer checks the computer, and it never gets tired, never gets generous, and never assumes it probably works because it worked yesterday.

Let me give you the scale of this in Echo, the audiobook study player this book uses as its worked example. Echo carries roughly seventeen hundred test annotations spread across three hundred sixty-one test files. Each annotation marks one check — one small "given this, expect that." Most of them are written in Swift Testing, which is Apple's modern test framework — you mark a function with an annotation that says "this is a test," and the framework finds it and runs it. There's also a shrinking remnant of eighteen files still on XCTest, the older framework Swift Testing is replacing — a little archaeological layer, migrated file by file as those tests get touched. Seventeen hundred checks. Dan didn't hand-write seventeen hundred checks; the agent wrote most of them, under direction. But every one of them runs, and every one of them is a claim about the app that gets re-proven on demand.

Now, the count sounds impressive, and honestly it's the least important number in this chapter. A pile of tests that all pass tells you less than you'd think, because there's a failure mode hiding in there: a test that could never fail. Tests get written after the code, against the code, and sometimes they quietly test nothing — they'd pass whether the feature worked or not. Which is why the habit matters more than the count: write the check first, and watch it fail on purpose. Before the fix exists, before the feature exists, you write the test that describes the correct behavior — and you run it, and you watch it go red. That red is not a setback; that red is the proof that your smoke detector has a battery in it. A test you never saw fail is a smoke detector you've never tested. It hangs on the ceiling looking reassuring, and you have no idea whether it would make a sound. Only after you've seen the test fail do you let the code be written that makes it pass — and now green means something, because you personally watched the difference between red and green.

So how do these seventeen hundred checks actually get run, on a modest Mac, dozens of times a day? With one word. The command is: make test. Two syllables. It's a memorized reflex at this point — the agent runs it, Dan runs it, it's the same word either way. The recipe behind that word lives in a file called the Makefile — a short file of named command recipes that sits at the root of the repository, so that long, fussy commands can be invoked by a single friendly name. And the recipe behind make test is genuinely fussy. It's worth unpacking, because every flag in it is a decision, and most of them are scars.

When you say make test, what actually runs is Apple's build tool, told to test the Echo scheme — and then a string of very specific choices. It targets an iPhone seventeen simulator, so the destination never has to be typed or remembered. It runs only the unit test target, deliberately skipping the slower user-interface tests, which get their own separate, less frequent run. It turns parallel testing off, so the tests run one suite at a time instead of fanning out across multiple simulators at once. It caps the number of compile jobs at five. And it skips code signing entirely — there's a build step in Echo that strips out pieces of a bundled machine-learning runtime the app doesn't use, and that stripping leaves behind husks the signing tool rejects; simulator tests don't need a signature anyway, so the recipe just turns signing off and sidesteps the whole fight.

Look at those choices as a set and you can read the machine they were made on. Parallel testing off, jobs capped at five — that's what a heavy build system looks like when it has learned to live politely on a Mac with sixteen gigabytes of memory. The tradeoff is real and Dan took it with open eyes: the test run is slower than it could be on a bigger machine, in exchange for a run that finishes reliably instead of drowning the computer halfway through. Slower and certain beat faster and flaky, and that was the whole negotiation.

But here is the actual point of the recipe, and it's not about memory. Verification you have to remember is verification you will skip. If running the tests meant recalling a destination string, a scheme name, a signing override, and two throttling flags, then on a tired Tuesday, after the fourth small change of the evening, the honest truth is the tests would not get run. Wrapped in one word, they get run every time — by Dan without thinking, and by the agent without being asked, because make test appears in the project's standing instructions as the thing you run before claiming anything works. The friction is the failure point. Remove the friction and the habit survives contact with a tired human. There's even a faster loop for the middle of a work session: a recipe called make build-tests compiles the test code once, and then a companion recipe runs just the one suite you're iterating on, so you're not rebuilding the world to re-ask one question. Same principle, smaller circle.

One more detail about how these tests stay honest. Echo's tests that touch storage don't touch the real database. They use the database service in an in-memory mode — a fresh, empty database that lives entirely in memory, exists only for the duration of the test, and vanishes when the test finishes. Every test starts from a known blank world, so a pass means the code works, not that the leftover data from the previous test happened to line up.

And for completeness, the unit tests aren't the whole picture — Echo actually has four test targets. The unit tests are the everyday workhorse, the ones make test runs. The user-interface tests, which drive the app the way a finger would, live in their own target and run separately, because they're slow and a little temperamental. And the watch tests compile with every build — so the code can't quietly rot — but actually run only when someone kicks them off by hand, because spinning up a watch simulator on this machine is an event, not a reflex. That's a spectrum decision, straight out of the last chapter: not every check earns a seat in the every-few-minutes loop. The cheap, fast, reliable checks run constantly; the expensive ones run deliberately. What matters is that the decision was made on purpose, once, and written into the recipes — not re-negotiated by a tired human at nine in the evening.

So that's mechanism one, and if your whole system were deterministic, you could stop there. But Echo's most interesting features aren't deterministic, and neither is the agent that builds them — which brings us to mechanism two: evaluations. Evals, for short. Evals verify the parts of the system where there is no single right answer to check against. Did the agent take the right trajectory of steps? Did it choose the right tools along the way? Did the final response meet the quality bar? These are real questions with real answers, but you can't answer them with an equals sign. Tests are checked by code. Evals are checked by labelled datasets — collections of examples where a human has already marked what good looks like — by scoring rubrics, written definitions of quality you can hold an output against, and by language-model judges, where one model grades another model's work against that rubric. It's the difference between grading a math quiz and grading an essay. The math quiz has an answer key. The essay needs a rubric and a reader — and the reader can be another model, as long as a human wrote the rubric and spot-checks the grades.

And then the paper says the hard thing, and it's worth saying slowly, because it draws the line most people don't want drawn: without both, the practice is always vibe coding — no matter how sophisticated the prompts are. Read that again at walking pace. You can have a beautiful prompt library, a meticulously tuned agent, a workflow that looks like engineering from across the room — and if the outputs aren't being verified by both mechanisms, the paper files you under vibe coding anyway. The prompts were never the differentiator. The proof is.

The paper splits evaluation one more useful way: output versus trajectory. Output evaluation checks the final artifact — does the code compile, do the tests pass, does the thing the agent handed you meet the bar. Trajectory evaluation checks how the agent got there — the full sequence of tool calls and intermediate reasoning. Did it actually run make test, or did it merely say it did? Did it read the file before editing it, or did it edit from a guess? You need both, and the paper's reason is one of the sharpest sentences in it: a fluent output that skipped its verification steps is a more dangerous failure than one with a visible error. A visible error stops you. A fluent, confident, wrong output sails past you — remember the persona from Chapter 2, the brilliant, slightly over-eager junior teammate. The junior who says "done, all tests passing" without having run the tests isn't lying, exactly; the words just came out in the shape that usually follows finished work. Scrolling back through the transcript to confirm the test command actually ran, and actually passed, is trajectory evaluation. It's thirty seconds, and it catches the failure that output checking structurally cannot.

Now — labelled datasets and model judges may sound like something only a lab with a budget does. So let me ground evals at solo scale, in Echo's fuzziest features, because a rubric and a habit is an eval even when nobody calls it one. Consider what Echo actually promises. It aligns an audiobook you own to its ebook so the words highlight as the narrator speaks them. Is the alignment correct? There's no function to assert on. The real question is: when the narrator says the word, does the highlight land on the word — close enough that your eye believes it? That's a quality bar, and the check is a human with the app open, reading along. Or take Echo's other headline feature: it narrates text-only ebooks entirely on-device, with twenty-eight built-in voices. Does a synthetic voice sound right? What number would even capture that?

Here's the story that made this real. An earlier version of Echo produced its narration audio in a compressed format, and that format introduced a faint, high-pitched whine underneath the voice. Every measurable number was fine. File sizes, durations, sample rates — every metric the code could check came back green. The tests passed, because the tests were checking the deterministic parts, and the deterministic parts were correct. The only instrument that caught the defect was a pair of ears — headphones on, actually listening to the output, the way a real user would. The fix was to move to a lossless format, trading larger files for clean audio, and the lesson outlived the fix: for Echo's fuzzy features, the ear is part of the test suite. A written rubric — the highlight lands within a beat of the spoken word; the voice carries no artifacts under headphones; pronunciation of names doesn't jar — plus a deliberate habit of spot-listening against it, is an evaluation program. Small, unglamorous, and it catches the class of bug that seventeen hundred passing tests are structurally deaf to.

Which brings this to the thing to do differently, starting with the next piece of work you touch. Never accept "it seems to work" for anything you intend to keep. That's the line. For throwaway code, vibe freely, with the paper's blessing. But the moment a change is destined for the version-controlled, shipping world, "seems right" is no longer an accepted currency. For deterministic behavior, the move is specific: ask the agent to write the failing test before the fix. Say it in those words — "write a test that fails because of this bug, show me the failure, then fix it." You define correct; the agent chases it. That single reordering changes your role from inspector of finished work — the weakest position, reviewing code you didn't write against a standard you never articulated — to author of the standard itself. And for fuzzy behavior, write the rubric down. Three sentences is enough. What does good mean for this feature? Where does the highlight have to land, how must the voice sound, what would make you wince? Then check against those sentences on purpose — not "does it seem fine," but "does it clear the bar I wrote before I was attached to the output." The rubric costs you two minutes. Its absence costs you a whine you shipped.

And notice what kind of change this is. It isn't a tool you install; it's a habit you install, and for a solo developer the habits are the culture. An agent dropped into a codebase where the tests run on one word, where the failing test comes before the fix, where the rubric exists in writing — that agent behaves like it works somewhere careful, because it does. The same agent dropped into a project with no recipes and no rubric behaves exactly like its surroundings: fluent, fast, and unverified. The model doesn't bring the discipline. It amplifies whichever discipline it finds.

So this chapter added real names to your vocabulary: Swift Testing, Apple's modern test framework, and its predecessor XCTest; the Makefile, the file of command recipes at the root of the repository; and make test, the one word that runs seventeen hundred proofs — plus the two mechanisms those tools serve, tests for the deterministic and evals for the fuzzy, checked as both output and trajectory. But notice what all this proof quietly assumes. The agent can only prove things inside a world it understands — it can only run make test if it knows make test exists, and it can only meet your quality bar if your quality bar was ever written where it could read it. What the agent knows, and how it comes to know it, is called context, and the next two chapters are about exactly that.
Chapter 6 — What the Agent Needs to Know

It is Monday afternoon, and Dan is at the kitchen table between shifts, teaching. The student is Claude Code, the agent that lives in his terminal, and the lesson is the house rules of Echo. Tests run against the in-memory database, never a real one. Commit messages follow a convention. Views stay thin, and the real logic lives in services where it can be tested. And on this Mac — sixteen gigabytes of memory, not one more — you never run two builds at the same time, because the second build will strangle the first and the whole machine will kneel. The agent takes all of this in graciously. It apologizes for the singleton it had been about to suggest. By the end of the session it is producing code that fits the project like it grew up there, and Dan closes the laptop feeling, reasonably, that progress was made.

Wednesday. New session. Same project, same kitchen table, same mug. And the very first suggestion out of the agent is a shared singleton — precisely the pattern Monday's lesson forbade — followed by a cheerful offer to run two builds in parallel to save time. Every rule from Monday is gone. Not weakened, not half-remembered. Gone.

Here is the thing to hold onto in that moment, because it is the difference between resentment and design: the agent has not betrayed you. It has simply forgotten, and it forgot on purpose. Every session starts blank. The context window — the agent's working memory, the only place your words ever land — is a whiteboard, and the whiteboard is wiped to a shine when the session ends. The model remembers its training. It does not remember your Tuesday. Nothing you explain in one conversation survives into the next unless something deliberately carries it across.

Your first instinct will be to repeat yourself better. Keep Monday's speech in a note somewhere, paste it in each morning, maybe sharpen the wording so it sticks harder this time. That instinct is understandable and wrong, because it answers a systems problem with willpower. You will forget to paste. You will paste an old version. You will spend the first ten minutes of every session re-teaching kindergarten. The fix is not a better speech, delivered more often. The fix is writing the rules down in a place the agent cannot miss — a file it reads automatically, at the start of every single session, before you have typed a word. The first edition of this book put it in one line, and the line still holds: you set the table once.
This chapter is about what goes on that table. And it turns out the industry has done a great deal of growing up on exactly this question.

The paper this book follows — the Google whitepaper on the new software development life cycle — names that maturation directly. As the field matured, it says, a key insight emerged: the quality of AI-generated code depends less on the cleverness of your prompts and more on the quality of the context provided. Sit with that sentence for a second, because it quietly demotes an entire cottage industry. For a couple of years the internet overflowed with prompt engineering tricks — magic phrasings, secret preambles, incantations that supposedly unlocked the model's hidden genius. Some of it worked, briefly, on some models. Most of it was a party trick, and like all party tricks it aged fast. What replaced it has a name the paper uses deliberately: context engineering — the practice of providing an AI agent with rich, structured information about your codebase, your architecture, your conventions, and your intent. Not a clever sentence. A well-stocked briefing.

And then the paper reframes the question in a way I want to give you almost word for word, because the reframe does more work than any technique in this chapter. The question is not, how do I trick the AI into writing good code. The question is, what would a new team member need to know to contribute effectively — and how do I encode that knowledge in a form the AI can use?

Notice what kind of question that is. It is not a wizard's question. It is an onboarding question — the kind a decent team lead asks before a new hire's first day. And that means you already know how to answer it, because you have been on the receiving end of it your whole working life. Your first day anywhere, somebody told you where things live, what the local customs are, and which door you must never open. Context engineering is writing that first-day briefing down, once, carefully, for a teammate who is brilliant, tireless, and arrives with total amnesia every single morning.

The paper sorts everything such a teammate might need into six types of context, and I want to walk through all six slowly, because each one maps onto a real, named artifact in Echo that you can go look at — or imitate.

First, instructions: the agent's core role, its goals, and its operational boundaries — who it is supposed to be while it works for you. Echo's project rules file, a file called CLAUDE.md that Claude Code reads automatically at the start of every session, opens by casting the agent as an expert, patient senior Apple-ecosystem developer mentoring a solo developer who is learning as he goes — and it requires the agent to explain why for every architectural choice it proposes. That opening paragraph is not decoration, and it is not flattery. Cast the agent as a code vending machine and you get code, take it or leave it. Cast it as a mentor obliged to show its reasoning, and every proposal arrives with the reasoning attached — which means every proposal arrives with something you can actually check. The role line is quietly doing verification work before a single test runs.

Second, knowledge: retrieved documents, architectural diagrams, domain-specific data — the reference shelf a new teammate would be pointed at. Echo's shelf has one big volume: a file called ARCHITECTURE.md, just over fifteen hundred lines, a map of the entire source tree — what each folder is for, which services own which responsibilities, how the pieces connect across all five surfaces the app ships on. Fifteen hundred lines sounds like a chore to maintain, and here is the detail worth stealing: nobody hand-writes them. The file is generated by one command, make architecture. Say it slowly: make, architecture. Make is a venerable old build tool being used here as a simple command runner, and architecture is the name of the recipe it runs — a script that walks the real source tree and rebuilds the map from what is actually there. The file even carries a warning at the top not to edit it by hand, because a hand-edited map drifts away from the territory, and a map that lies is worse than no map at all.

Third, memory. The paper splits this one in two: short-term memory is the session log — what just happened, what was tried, what failed — and long-term memory is persistent state — what the project is, what was decided months ago and why. Echo handles the long-term half with a dedicated memory folder, and at the top of that folder sits a file called MEMORY.md that works as an index: one line per memory, each line pointing at a fuller note filed beside it. The shape matters more than the contents. The agent can skim a one-line-per-item index in a single breath, cheaply, on every session — and pull the full note only when a task actually touches that topic. An index the agent can afford to always read, backed by detail it only pays for when needed.

Fourth, examples: reference patterns, demonstrations — showing rather than telling. Echo's rules do something that looks almost lazy and works remarkably well. Instead of describing the project's dependency-injection style in three abstract paragraphs, they point at one named, living example — the database service, a real service in the codebase that shows the entire pattern in miniature, including how tests swap in an in-memory version of it instead of touching a real database — and they say, in effect: follow that. One good worked example beats a page of abstract description, for the same reason it does with a human junior. Nobody learns a house style from the style guide. They learn it from the one file everyone says to go read.

Fifth, tools: the precise definitions of what the agent can invoke — the scripts, the commands, the external services, and exactly what each one does. In Echo this runs from the humble to the elaborate. The humble end is make test. Again, slowly: make, test — one command that runs the project's roughly seventeen hundred automated tests the same way every time, no judgment calls, no forgotten flags. The elaborate end is the bridge that lets the agent drive Xcode — Apple's build system — to compile and test the actual apps. The common thread is precision. A tool the agent knows about, exactly, is a tool the agent will reach for; a tool described vaguely might as well not exist.

And sixth, guardrails: hard constraints, formatting rules, safety validations — the rules that hold even when the agent is having a bad day, because they are enforced by machinery rather than requested by prose. In Echo that machinery is a set of hooks — small programs that intercept the agent's actions and can block the dangerous ones outright — and they matter enough to get a chapter of their own soon. For now, just place them on the shelf with the other five: the sixth kind of context is the kind that does not ask.

One practical wrinkle before we move on, because you will hit it in your first week: Claude Code actually reads two rule files, not one. There is a global CLAUDE.md that lives in your home directory, inside a hidden folder called dot claude, and its rules apply to every project on the machine. Dan's global file carries the things that are true of Dan rather than of any one app: the mentor role and tone he wants from the agent everywhere, his defaults for how work gets split among subagents, and the sixteen-gigabyte build law — because that law follows the Mac, not the project. Then each project carries its own CLAUDE.md at its root, holding only what is true there: this codebase's conventions, this project's named example service, this repository's workflow. The split keeps both files honest. If a rule belongs to the person or the machine, it goes global; if it belongs to the codebase, it stays local, and neither file has to repeat the other.

There is also a vendor-neutral cousin worth knowing by name: a file called AGENTS.md — the same idea under a banner no single company owns, which a growing number of coding agents can read. Echo keeps one, adapted from a public template published by Paul Hudson, a well-known Swift educator, with the credit noted right in the project's README. If you ever switch tools, AGENTS.md is the version of your briefing that travels with you.

Now the part that separates a rule file that works from a rule file that merely exists. If you opened Echo's CLAUDE.md expecting a grand, comprehensive style guide, you would be disappointed. It is short, and it is oddly specific, and both of those qualities are the point — because every rule in the file traces back to a specific wound.

The most instructive scar in there is a history note. It records that, at one point, an elaborate protocol-abstraction layer was built into Echo — a whole tier of Swift interfaces designed to make everything swappable and mockable, someday, in some imagined future where flexibility would pay off. Nothing ever used it. The mock objects sat orphaned. The someday never came. A few weeks ago, after a full code audit, the entire layer was deleted — and the rules file gained a standing order in its place: add a protocol back only when a real second implementation actually exists. Notice what that rule is not. It is not a soft aspiration like prefer simple designs where appropriate. It is hard, checkable, and specific, and the corpse of the deleted layer is cited right there as the reason. The same is true across the file. The sixteen-gigabyte rules — never parallel testing, never uncapped build jobs, never two concurrent builds — each clause is the residue of an afternoon this particular Mac spent frozen. The dependency-injection convention, the required commit format — line after line, you can feel the incident behind it.

Rules are earned, not composed. A rule file written in one ambitious sitting is a wish list — it describes the developer you would like to be, in prose the agent will struggle to act on. A rule file grown one wound at a time is a fence built exactly where the cliff is. And keeping it short is not a stylistic preference; it is an operating cost. Every line of that file is read by the agent on every turn, spending its attention and your tokens both. Ten sharp rules that all still bleed a little are worth more than a hundred aspirational ones the agent must wade through to find the two that matter today.

There is a bigger idea hiding inside the scar principle, and it is one of this book's throughlines: AI amplifies the culture it lands in, and for a solo developer, the culture is your habits. A rule file is the first place your culture stops being a mood and becomes an artifact — your actual working values, written down, versioned in git, and read aloud to your tireless junior teammate at the start of every shift. Which habits made the file tells you, honestly, what your culture really is.

All of which brings us to the paper's first recommended practice for individual developers, and I will hand it to you nearly word for word, because it needs no translation: set up an AGENTS.md — or the equivalent for your agent of choice, which for Claude Code means a CLAUDE.md — for your project. Today. Start with ten lines: stack, conventions, hard rules, workflow. That really is the assignment. Ten lines. What languages and frameworks this project uses. How code is shaped here — the two or three conventions that actually define your style. The hard rules — the things that must never happen, whatever the reason. And the workflow — how a change travels from idea to commit in this repository. If your project has a test command, one of the ten lines names it — run make test before claiming anything works — because, as you now know, a tool the agent knows about precisely is a tool the agent uses. You can write those ten lines before your coffee cools, and the agent will read them at the start of every session for the rest of the project's life.

Then adopt the habit that matters more than the file itself. The paper's phrasing: add a rule every time the agent does something it should not do again. That single habit is the entire maintenance model. The agent reaches for a pattern you have banned — you stop, and you add one line. It runs steps in the wrong order — one line. It touches a folder it should have left alone — one line. The file grows the way scar tissue grows: only where there was a cut. And do the other half too, the half almost everyone skips: prune. When a rule stops earning its keep — the tool it guarded is gone, the convention it enforced has been retired — delete the line, because you are paying for it on every turn whether it helps or not. A rule file is a garden, not a monument.

Do this, and you have installed the first real machine in your factory. That is another throughline of this book: your product is not just the code, it is the system that produces the code — and a rules file is that system's foundation stone. Small, boring, and load-bearing.

So, the vocabulary you carry out of this chapter: context engineering, the practice that replaced prompt tricks, and its six types of context — instructions, knowledge, memory, examples, tools, and guardrails — pinned in Echo to a project CLAUDE.md and a global one, to ARCHITECTURE.md and the make architecture command that regenerates it, to the MEMORY.md index, to the database service held up as the pattern to follow, to make test, and to the hooks waiting a few chapters ahead. You now know what the agent needs to know. The harder design question is when it should know it — which context deserves to be bolted into every session, and which should wait quietly on a shelf until a task actually calls for it. That boundary, between what always loads and what loads on demand, is exactly where we go next.
Chapter 7 — Static, Dynamic, and Skills

Picture the moment a bad habit starts. The agent does something mildly infuriating — it names a branch wrong, or declares victory without running the tests — and you know exactly how to fix it forever. You open CLAUDE.md, your project's standing rule file, and you add a paragraph. There. Done. It feels like writing a memo once so you never have to give the speech again, and it feels completely free.

The first edition of this book warned you that it is not free. Every extra paragraph in your rule file is a tax you pay forever, on every single turn. At the time, that line was folk wisdom — a hunch, the feeling of a taxi meter ticking somewhere just out of sight. Because that is what actually happens: the agent takes the entire rule file with it into every conversation and keeps it in view on every turn, so a paragraph that helps you once a month is being paid for hundreds of times a week, on tasks that have nothing to do with it. Dan could feel that cost the way you feel a coat getting heavier in the rain. What he couldn't do, back then, was name it.

The paper names it. It draws a clean line through everything an agent knows and calls the two sides static context and dynamic context. And it goes further than naming. It says the boundary between the two is not housekeeping, not a tuning knob for enthusiasts — it is a first-class architectural decision, the kind you review and version like any other piece of your system. The hunch got an engineering name and a price tag. That line — what belongs on each side of it, why the two sides cost such different amounts, and the pattern the paper crowns for managing the cheap side — is where this chapter is headed. And that pattern, a mechanism called Agent Skills, happens to be producing the very words you are hearing right now. We'll get there.

Start with static context. Static context is everything that is always loaded — present in every interaction before you've typed a single word of your actual request. The paper's inventory is short and worth hearing in full. First, system instructions: the standing orders the tool itself ships with, the ones you mostly never see. Second, rule files: a file called AGENTS.md, which has become a vendor-neutral convention that many coding tools read; a file called CLAUDE.md, the one Claude Code reads — think of it as your project's house rules, written down once so they're followed every time; and their cousins, like GEMINI.md for Google's tools. Same idea, different nameplate on the door. Third, global memory — the notes an agent keeps about you and your preferences across sessions. And fourth, persona definitions — descriptions of who the agent is supposed to be while it works.

Notice what those four things have in common. None of them is about the task. All of them are about the worker. Static context defines who the agent is and how it behaves — its identity, its standards, its non-negotiables. And it is expensive for a precise reason, which the paper states flatly: every token is present in every interaction, regardless of relevance. A token, remember, is roughly a word-sized piece of text that the model reads and that you pay for. So your carefully written rule about database migrations rides along, at full price, while the agent adjusts the color of a button. Your branching policy attends every conversation about typos. Nothing gets skipped, because nothing can be skipped. Always loaded means always loaded.

Dynamic context is the other side of the line: everything that loads on demand. Skill instructions that arrive only when a task matches them — much more on that shortly. Tool results retrieved during execution, like the output of a test run, which shows up when the test actually runs and not a moment before. Documents fetched at the instant they're needed rather than stapled to every request. And windowed session history — the recent slice of your conversation, with older turns falling away behind you like scenery out a train window. Dynamic context is efficient for the mirror-image reason: the agent pays the token cost only when the information is needed.

If you want one picture to hold onto, take a tradesperson. Static context is the tool belt. Whatever hangs on the hip gets carried up every ladder and into every crawl space, all day, every day, whether the job needs it or not. Dynamic context is the van parked outside. The tile saw and the long ladder wait out there rent-free until a job actually calls for them, and then it's a short walk. Nobody sensible carries the tile saw on their belt. And yet plenty of us, in our first months with an agent, wrote rule files that did exactly that.

Because here is the tension, and the paper states it plainly enough that I'll give it to you nearly word for word. Too much static context wastes tokens and dilutes signals. Too little means the agent forgets critical rules. The best systems treat this boundary as a first-class architectural decision, reviewed and versioned like any other configuration.

Both halves of that deserve a breath. Wasting tokens you already understand — that's the meter. Diluting signals is subtler, and arguably worse. An agent's attention is finite, and every rule in the always-loaded pile competes with every other rule for a share of it. Ten rules land like ten commandments. Fifty rules land like terms and conditions — technically present, faithfully scrolled past. When Dan's rule file was at its bloated worst, the agent didn't fail by ignoring CLAUDE.md; it failed by weighting some middle paragraph about code style roughly the same as the line that said never push to the main branch. Past a certain size, more static context doesn't just cost more. It makes the rules that matter land softer.

The other half — too little means the agent forgets critical rules — is why you can't fix bloat by deleting cheerfully. A rule moved out of static context is only obeyed when something loads it back in at the right moment. That is the one real trade in this chapter, so let's name it once and cleanly: static placement buys certainty at a permanent price; dynamic placement buys thrift at the risk of a rule that never gets loaded when it mattered. Neither side is the virtuous side. The judgment call is deciding which rules deserve certainty.

Now translate reviewed and versioned like any other configuration for a team of one at a kitchen table. It means your CLAUDE.md is load-bearing infrastructure, not a diary. When you change it, look at the change the way you'd look at a code change: what does this paragraph add to every future conversation, what does it push down in the agent's attention, is it worth a permanent seat? Dan keeps his rule files in version control alongside everything else, and a change to CLAUDE.md gets at least the scrutiny of a change to the app — arguably more, because a bad line of app code breaks one feature, while a bad line of rule file quietly degrades every session from now on.

So the discipline becomes: identity stays static, and everything else needs somewhere cheaper to live. The pattern the paper crowns for that somewhere is Agent Skills — in the paper's own words, structured, portable packages of procedural knowledge that the agent loads only when the task calls for it. Strip the jargon and a skill is a folder containing written instructions for one kind of chore. A recipe card filed on a shelf, instead of a recipe pinned permanently to the kitchen wall where it blocks the light.

The mechanism that keeps skills cheap is called progressive disclosure, and it is the best idea in this chapter. At startup, the agent does not read your skills. It sees only lightweight metadata — a name and a one-line description for each skill on the shelf. Think of walking past a spice rack and reading nothing but the labels. When a task matches — when what you've asked for lines up with what a description promises — the agent loads that one skill's full instructions. And deep reference material — long specifications, exhaustive examples — comes only when explicitly needed, a third layer down. The result, as the paper puts it, is that an agent can carry dozens of specialized capabilities while paying the token cost for only the one it is actively using. The agent stays a lightweight generalist that flexes into a specialist on demand. The tool belt stays light, the van stays stocked, and — this is the part the belt-and-van picture undersells — fetching from the van happens automatically, triggered by nothing more than the match between your request and a one-line label.

The paper credits skills with solving four distinct problems, and it's worth hearing all four, because each one probably describes a week of your life. First, context rot from overloaded prompts — the slow silting-up of rule files we've spent half this chapter on; skills are where the silt drains to. Second, the absence of procedural memory. A model does not remember how you like things done. Every session, it is brilliant, tireless, and brand new on its first day — that over-eager junior teammate, meeting your release process for the hundredth first time. A skill is procedural memory written down where the newcomer will reliably find it. Third, the operational overhead of multi-agent architectures. Before skills, the usual way to get specialist behavior was a fleet of separately configured specialist agents, each one its own thing to maintain; one generalist with a shelf of skills is one thing to maintain. And fourth, portability across tools and vendors. A skill is just files in a folder, so it moves when you move — between projects, between machines, even between one vendor's tool and another's — in a way that a pile of tool-specific settings never will.

Enough theory. Let's open the actual shelves. Inside the Echo repository, in a skills folder under the hidden dot-claude directory, there are exactly three project skills — and the number itself is the first lesson. The shelf is curated, not hoarded.

The first is called doc-sync. Echo's CLAUDE.md carries a mandate marked critical: when the code changes, the living documents change with it — ARCHITECTURE.md, README.md, CHANGELOG.md, and ROADMAP.md, the four files that describe what Echo is and where it's going. But that mandate is identity — the what, one standing line of law. The how — check these four files in this order, and here are ready-to-paste snippets shaped for each one — is procedure, and it lives in doc-sync, loaded only in the minutes before a pull request goes up. One sentence in the rule file, a full checklist on the shelf. That is the static-dynamic boundary drawn through a real project, and you can hear how little it cost.

The second is echo-manual-epub. Echo ships its own user manual inside the app — a narrated ebook called Welcome to Echo, The Listener's Manual, which the app reads aloud to you, because of course it does; reading ebooks aloud is what Echo is for. Rebuilding that manual is a fussy, many-step chore that happens a few times a year. That's perfect skill material: too rare to memorize, too fiddly to improvise, and utterly useless in every session that isn't about the manual. echo-manual-epub is the reason the rebuild goes right in November exactly the way it went right back in June.

The third is new-schema-migration, and it earns a closer look. It scaffolds the next migration for Echo's database — Echo uses a library called GRDB, a Swift-friendly layer over the SQLite database — and migrations are the kind of chore with famous failure modes: two migrations colliding on the same version number, or a migration written carefully and then never registered, so it silently never runs. The skill exists to make both mistakes hard. But notice what else Dan did. new-schema-migration is deliberately locked, using a setting whose name says exactly what it does — disable model invocation, set to true. With that set, the agent can never decide on its own that some task matches this skill. It runs only when Dan asks for it by name. A migration rearranges the ground your data stands on, and the first edition's rule still holds: the riskier the chore, the narrower the trigger.

Those are the project shelves. Dan's global shelf — the same dot-claude arrangement, but in his home directory, following him into every project — holds about twenty more skill folders. Among them: swift-testing-pro, for writing tests with Swift's modern testing framework; ios-code-audit, which sweeps a codebase for problems; xcode-build-orchestrator, which shepherds build optimization on a modest sixteen-gigabyte Mac; audiobook-library, which keeps his audiobook collection organized; and one called explainer-audiobook — which is, at this very moment, the skill producing the words in your ears. This book is a run of a skill on that shelf. And because a shelf that useful is worth protecting, Echo's repository root carries a small file called skills-lock.json that pins each skill to a known version — the shelf itself is under version control, reviewed like any other configuration, which is exactly what the paper prescribes.

How does a shelf like that grow to twenty? By one rule, unchanged since the first edition: the moment you catch yourself giving the same multi-step instruction for the second time, stop and write it down as a skill. Not the fifth time. The second. Twice is a pattern, a pattern is procedure, and procedure belongs on the shelf, not in your patience.

Which brings us to what you do differently, starting this week. Open your rule file — your CLAUDE.md, or your AGENTS.md, whichever your tool reads — and go down it slowly, asking one question of every line: is this identity, or is this procedure? Identity is who the agent is, plus the hard rules that must survive every session with certainty — never push to the main branch, always run the tests, never invent an API that doesn't exist. Identity stays static and pays the tax gladly, because the alternative — a safety rule that didn't happen to load — costs far more. Procedure is how to perform some particular chore: the release steps, the migration recipe, the documentation check. Procedure moves out, into a skill that loads on demand. Most rule files that have been growing for six months turn out to be two-thirds procedure wearing identity's clothes. Dan's was. Yours probably is too. When you're done, the file should read less like a manual and more like a creed — short, sharp, and true in every single session.

And once you own a shelf of skills, you inherit the maintenance that comes with ownership. Watch for skill rot. A stale skill does not fail loudly. It fails by being followed. If a chore changes shape — a folder moves, a step becomes unnecessary, a tool gets swapped out — and the skill still describes the old world, the agent won't complain, won't hesitate, won't notice. It will execute your obsolete instructions with total confidence, because written-down procedure is precisely the thing it trusts most. That's what makes rot on the shelf sneakier than bloat in the rule file: bloat you pay for visibly, on every turn, until the pain makes you act. A stale skill sleeps quietly and for free, right up until the day it wakes up wrong. So when you change how a chore works, update its skill the same day, while the difference is still fresh in your hands — the same reflex doc-sync enforces for Echo's living documents. Code changed, words change with it, before the work is called done.

You now hold the vocabulary this chapter came to teach. Static context is the always-loaded identity layer — system instructions, rule files like CLAUDE.md and AGENTS.md, memory, persona — where every token is paid for in every interaction. Dynamic context is everything fetched on demand and paid for only when needed. Agent Skills are portable folders of procedural knowledge, kept cheap by progressive disclosure — label first, full instructions on a match, deep reference only when asked — the same mechanism that let explainer-audiobook sit silently on a shelf until the day it was asked to write this book. The paper's companion piece on the subject, a paper on context engineering covering sessions, skills, and memory, closes with a line worth keeping: context engineering is the bridge between vibe coding and agentic engineering. What the agent knows is now, formally, something you design. Next, we zoom all the way out — from what the agent knows to what all of this does to the shape of a software project itself: the software development life cycle, end to end.
Chapter 8 — The New Life Cycle

Say the old rhythm out loud, the way you might recall a season you lived through. Requirements, two or three days. Design, a day or two. Implementation, one to three weeks. Testing, three to five days. Review and deploy, two or three days. And then maintenance, which is not a phase so much as a climate — it simply goes on, forever, as long as the software is alive. If you ever worked anywhere near a software team, or even just read about how they work, that rhythm is familiar the way a school timetable is familiar. The sprint was measured in weeks. The phases were fences: work moved from one pen to the next, a ticket sliding across a board, a handoff, a meeting, another handoff. You could stand at the fence line and watch a feature graze its way through requirements, into design, into the long middle pasture of implementation, and eventually out the far gate into production. And the middle pasture was the big one. One to three weeks of implementation, against days for everything else. Implementation was where the calendar went to die. It was the reason estimates existed, the reason project managers existed, the reason the phrase "how long will this take" carried a faint note of dread. Now look at that list of numbers again, because one of them has quietly collapsed. Implementation — the one-to-three-week pasture, the widest part of the whole field — has shrunk from weeks to hours. Not for everything, and not without conditions, but for a large and growing share of real work, the part of the job that used to be the job now finishes before lunch. And here is the thing you need to sit with: when one wall of a house shrinks, you do not get a smaller house with the same floor plan. You get a different house. The hallways no longer line up. The rooms that used to be far apart are suddenly adjacent. Doors open onto spaces that used to be somewhere else entirely. The software development life cycle — the formal name for that old rhythm, often shortened to S-D-L-C — is the house, and its biggest room just contracted by an order of magnitude or two. This chapter is about the new floor plan. Not a pep talk about speed, and not a eulogy for the old ways, but an honest walk through what the shape of the work actually looks like when the middle of it stops being the bottleneck — and what becomes the bottleneck instead. Because something always is. Compression never makes constraints disappear; it relocates them. The interesting question is never "did we get faster" but "where did the waiting move," and the answer, as you will hear, is that the waiting moved somewhere much closer to you.

Before we walk the new house, it is worth remembering that this is not the first renovation. The software development life cycle has already survived one revolution in living memory, and knowing that story gives this one some depth. For roughly two decades, most of the industry migrated — slowly, argumentatively, one retrospective at a time — from sequential waterfall processes to iterative ones. Waterfall was the original fence-line model taken to its logical extreme: gather every requirement first, design everything second, build everything third, test everything fourth, and pray that nothing you learned in month nine invalidated a decision from month one. It failed often enough, and expensively enough, that the industry spent twenty years replacing it. In came Agile sprints — short, repeating cycles of a week or two, each delivering something small and real. In came continuous integration, which is the practice of merging and building everyone's work constantly, many times a day, instead of saving up a terrifying pile of changes for the end. In came DevOps pipelines — automation that carries code from a developer's commit all the way to running servers without a human ceremonially carrying floppy disks between departments. In came rapid release trains, shipping every week or every day instead of every year. That whole revolution had one underlying theme: shorten the feedback loop. Move testing closer to the work. Find out you are wrong on Tuesday instead of in the fall. It was a real transformation, and it took twenty years, and the industry is genuinely better for it. The paper — the Google whitepaper this book is built around — opens its life-cycle chapter by honoring exactly that history, and then it makes a sharper observation about what is happening now. AI compresses this cycle dramatically, the paper says, but unevenly. That word, unevenly, is the one doing the work. Implementation that once took weeks can now be done in hours. But requirements, architecture, and verification remain stubbornly human-paced. Deciding what to build still takes as long as deciding takes. Choosing a sound structure still takes as long as judgment takes. And checking that the thing actually works — really checking, not glancing — still takes as long as attention takes. Picture a highway that gets widened from two lanes to ten, but only in the middle stretch. The middle flows beautifully now. And all the congestion, every bit of it, relocates to the on-ramps and the off-ramps — the places where human decisions feed work in and human judgment takes work out. The Agile revolution shortened the loop. This one warps it, stretching some parts to near-instant while leaving others exactly as slow as human thought. A warped loop is not a faster version of the old loop. It behaves differently, and it rewards different habits.

So what does the warped loop actually look like? The paper is careful here, and its language is worth taking at close to full strength. The result, it says, is not a faster version of the old software development life cycle. It is a different workflow. The boundaries between phases blur — you are no longer cleanly "in design" or "in testing," because the agent can sketch, build, and test in a single conversational breath. Iteration cycles shorten from weeks to minutes; you can propose a change, watch it built, watch it tested, and reject it before your coffee cools. Specifications stop being documents that gather dust after kickoff and become eval criteria — living checklists that the output is actually measured against, the fuzzy cousin of a test suite that you met back in chapter five. The agent self-corrects: it runs the tests, reads its own failures, and fixes its own mistakes for several rounds before a human ever needs to look. Verification itself splits in two — evaluating the output, meaning the finished code, and evaluating the trajectory, meaning the path the agent took to get there. And through all of it, the developer's role shifts from primary implementor to system designer and quality arbiter. You stop being the person who lays the bricks and become the person who draws the plans and inspects the walls. Quality arbiter is a title worth taking seriously, too — an arbiter does not merely glance and nod, an arbiter rules, and a ruling requires evidence: tests run, behavior observed, the diff actually read. Then the paper compresses the entire shift into one sentence, and this is the sentence to sit with on a long walk. We are no longer waiting on human hands to type boilerplate; we are waiting on human minds to define the boundaries. Read that again, slowly, because both halves matter. The first half is the celebrated part — the typing bottleneck is gone, the boilerplate writes itself, the pasture emptied out. The second half is the invoice. The waiting did not end; it moved to the definition of boundaries — to knowing, precisely, what should be built, what should not be touched, and what "done" is going to mean before anyone starts. In the old world, a vague specification was survivable, because a human implementor spent three weeks inside the problem and quietly patched the vagueness with their own judgment as they went. In the new world there is no three-week soak. The agent builds your vague specification in ninety minutes, vagueness and all, at full speed and with complete confidence. Specification quality is the new bottleneck. That is the paper's phrase and it earns its place: the quality ceiling of what comes out is now set, almost entirely, by the clarity of what went in. Which raises the obvious, practical question — what does a specification good enough to be the bottleneck actually look like? Not in a slide deck. In a real project, on a real kitchen table.

Here is one, from Echo — the audiobook study player you have been following through this book, built by one developer at a kitchen table by directing Claude Code, the AI coding agent that runs in the terminal. The task was the player-screen redesign: reshaping the screen a listener actually lives on, the one with playback, the transcript, the controls. Under the old rhythm this is a multi-week job with fences all the way down. Under the new one, the shape of the work inverted, and you can see the inversion in the artifact that drove it. The specification was a plan document a little over two thousand two hundred lines long. Let that number land — the plan for the feature was the size of a small book chapter collection, far larger than many of the code changes it produced. And its structure is a portrait of boundary-definition in the wild. At the very top sat a section of locked decisions — choices made once, in writing, before any building began, so they could never be silently renegotiated mid-task. One example: the loop control, the control for repeating a stretch of audio, stays a three-way control — that was settled — but it relocates to a slide-up panel. Decided, recorded, done. Below that came an explicit out-of-scope list, and this part matters more than it sounds. The watch app: untouched. The lock-screen and headphone controls: marked do-not-touch. The CarPlay integration: do-not-touch. Remember the agent's persona — a brilliant, tireless, slightly over-eager junior teammate. It will do exactly what you asked, including the parts you did not actually mean. An out-of-scope list is how you fence off the parts you did not mean before the eager teammate helpfully renovates them too. Then the work itself: split into five independent workstreams, grouped into waves ordered by dependency, so that streams with no overlap could run without colliding and nothing started before the thing it needed. And the detail that ties this chapter to chapter five: every task — about two dozen of them — carried a verification gate, a concrete check that had to pass before the task counted as done, paired with a commit message written in advance. Writing the commit message before the work is a quiet trick with sharp edges: if you cannot describe the finished change in one clean sentence ahead of time, the task is not actually defined yet. The implementation — the part that used to be the whole job — took hours per wave. The human time pooled at the two ends: days of thinking poured into the plan on one side, careful review on the other. That is the honest tradeoff, named once: the twenty-two-hundred-line plan cost real evenings that the old way would have spent typing. The cost did not vanish. It moved upstream, where a mistake costs a sentence instead of a rewrite. That is the new life cycle, lived at kitchen-table scale.

And here is the part of the new life cycle that is easiest to underestimate: the cycle keeps turning even when no one is home. The paper's picture of the AI-driven life cycle lists continuous automation as a standing feature, not a finale — and in Echo, continuous automation has a schedule you could set a watch by. Every Monday, a scheduled workflow named devlog-update wakes up on its own, regenerates Echo's development log — the running public diary of what changed and why — and opens a pull request with the result into the nightly branch, the integration branch where fresh work lands first. No one asks it to. Dan is out walking his mail route while a robot writes up the week and files the paperwork. And every morning at five twenty, Halifax time, a second workflow called release-trains builds whatever is on the nightly branch and ships it to TestFlight — Apple's beta distribution service, the system that puts pre-release builds on real testers' phones — delivering to a small internal group called Nightly, capped at one hundred testers. Once a week, on Mondays, a bigger train leaves the station: an external TestFlight release to a group that can hold up to ten thousand testers, with Apple's Beta App Review checking the first build of each new version. Even the odd departure time is a small engineering decision worth savoring: five twenty, not five o'clock, because the world's scheduled jobs overwhelmingly fire on the hour, and a run scheduled at twenty past dodges the on-the-hour cron herd — cron being the ancient, reliable Unix scheduler that runs commands on a timetable. And the trains are built to fail gracefully: if the code-signing secrets ever go missing, the run degrades to a compile-only build rather than a failure — a rule the project states as no red-X nightlies, because a wall of red failure marks teaches you to ignore the wall, and an ignored signal is worse than no signal. Twenty years ago this apparatus — nightly builds, tester distribution, automated release notes, staged review — was the mark of a company with a release engineering team and a budget to match. Today it is one person's cron schedule, a few text files that fire on a timetable while their author sleeps or delivers the mail. Continuous automation is not a corporate luxury. It is the part of your factory that runs without you, and it is exactly as available to a solo developer at a kitchen table as it is to a campus full of engineers.

One more thing the paper says, and it says it about itself, which is why it is worth passing on. The phase-by-phase picture it draws — the one this chapter has been walking through — reflects the state of things as of mid twenty twenty-six, and the authors say plainly that it is shifting fast. Early signs suggest the compression will spread beyond implementation. Teams are already experimenting with workflows that go directly from specs to review — the agent handling implementation, testing, and deployment in between, entire fences simply gone. The boundaries drawn in this chapter may look different in twelve months, and the paper says so in nearly those words. That humility is not a weakness in the framework; it is the reason to trust it. But notice what the authors put on the other side of the ledger, the part they expect to hold: human judgment, taste, and the skill to verify AI output. The rooms will keep moving. The person who decides what to build and checks what got built does not go away — that role concentrates. Which points at something you can actually do, starting tomorrow, and it costs nothing but honesty. For one week, track where your hours actually go. Nothing elaborate — a note on your phone with three buckets: before, middle, and after. Before is deciding what to build: specs, plans, locked decisions, out-of-scope fences. Middle is implementation — typing code yourself, or shepherding an agent line by line, nudging, re-prompting, hovering. After is verification: reviewing diffs, running tests, actually using the feature. Each time you switch, jot the time. At the end of the week, add it up and look at the shape. If most of your hours still sit in the middle bucket — and for most of us, the first honest week says they do — you are staffing the pasture that already emptied, personally guarding the one wall of the house that shrank. Move the hours deliberately. Upstream, into specification: next feature, spend one more evening on the plan than feels natural — lock the decisions, write the out-of-scope list, write the commit message before the work. Downstream, into verification: give the review real, unhurried attention instead of the tired glance left over after implementation ate the day. You now have the shape of the new life cycle in your hands: a warped loop where implementation runs in hours, specification quality is the new bottleneck, plans carry locked decisions and verification gates, and workflows like devlog-update and release-trains keep the wheel turning through TestFlight while you sleep. Next, we take that same transformation phase by phase — requirements, design, implementation, testing, review, maintenance — and put numbers on each one.
Chapter 9 — Phase by Phase

There is a cartoon that has hung on software office walls for decades. It shows a tire swing hanging from a tree branch, drawn over and over: what the customer described, what the project lead understood, what the engineer built, what the customer was billed for — every panel a stranger, more baroque contraption than the last. And then the final panel: what the customer actually needed. A plain tire on a plain rope. The joke has outlived every methodology fad because the wound never closes. Requirements — the phase where somebody's need becomes somebody's plan — has historically been the place where the gap between what stakeholders want and what engineers build is widest, because translating a business need into a technical specification was manual, slow, and error-prone. Every hop in that telephone game lost a little truth.

This is the phase the paper says is changing first, and changing hardest. Modern AI tools now participate directly in requirements refinement. Hand one a product brief and it will generate user stories — those short, plain sentences describing what a user needs to do and why. It will spot edge cases humans miss, because it has effectively read the failure reports of a thousand similar systems: what happens at midnight, what happens offline, what happens when the list is empty. Describe a service in ordinary English and it will produce interface schemas — the formal contracts two programs use to talk to each other — without you writing a line. And hand it a whole specification document, and it will build an interactive prototype in minutes: something you can click and hold, not just read. The feedback loop from requirements to prototype, which used to take weeks, collapses to nearly zero. Which sets up the paper's line, and it is worth hearing slowly: requirements stop being a document handed off between teams. They become a conversation between humans and AI that produces specification and initial implementation simultaneously. You describe what you want. The machine drafts something real. You point at the draft and say, not that — this. The pointing is the requirement now, and it is a more honest requirement than any document ever was, because you are reacting to a thing that exists. People are unreliable at describing what they want in the abstract and remarkably reliable at recognizing what is wrong with a thing in front of them — and this new shape of the phase plays directly to that strength. The misunderstandings that used to surface after three months of building now surface after ten minutes of clicking, while they are still cheap.

Echo's version of this phase is instructive precisely because Dan has no stakeholders to disappoint but himself — and it turns out the gap survives even then, as the gap between what you vaguely wanted and what you actually asked for. His answer is a plan anyone can go read today: the road to version one point oh, laid out in the README file — the front-page document of the repository — and built around six competitive wedges: the study moat, rock-solid reliability, clarity, trust, sync done right, and support. The plan is gate-driven. Each wedge carries conditions that must be true before the app can call itself version one point oh, and it ships when it is deep and solid, not on a date. Because it lives in the README instead of a drawer, the agent can read it at the start of any session. That is requirements as a living conversation — a specification that never gets to quietly go stale.

Then comes design and architecture, and if you are expecting the paper to say the machines are eating this phase too, it says nearly the opposite, and it says it bluntly: architecture remains the most stubbornly human-centric phase of the software development life cycle, and for good reason. Architectural decisions are fundamentally about trade-offs. Consistency versus availability — should every device show the same answer at the same instant, or should the system keep working when a connection drops? Complexity versus flexibility — do you build the simple thing that fits today, or the general thing that fits futures you can only guess at? Build versus buy — do you write it yourself or adopt someone else's? None of these questions has a right answer. They have right-for-you answers, and right-for-you depends on business context, organizational constraints, and long-term strategy — things the AI cannot fully grasp, because they do not live in the code. They live in your market, your finances, and your appetite for risk.

What AI excels at is implementing architectural decisions once they are made. Echo — the audiobook study player this book keeps returning to — offers two clean examples. When Dan needed a database layer, Apple offered him SwiftData, the first-party framework: integrated, convenient, included with the tools. He chose instead a library called GRDB — a Swift toolkit wrapped around SQLite, the small, battle-tested database engine found inside countless phones and apps. That was his call, and he made it for control and testability: with GRDB he can create a real database in memory inside a test, run every migration against it, and prove that the data layer behaves — the kind of proof a developer with roughly seventeen hundred automated tests has learned to want. The trade was real and he paid it once: he gave up the convenience of Apple's own framework, the smooth road of staying first-party, in exchange for a data layer he can see all the way through. The second example: on the Mac, Echo's player is built around the Mac player model, a component kept deliberately independent of the iPhone's playback stack rather than sharing its machinery. Also Dan's call. And here is the division of labor the paper describes: once those decisions existed, the agent implemented them across hundreds of files, each new piece conforming to the established pattern. The developer's role shifts from writing boilerplate — the repetitive scaffolding code every feature needs — to making and documenting the structural decisions that boilerplate implements. You decide where the walls go. The machine pours the concrete, and it pours it exactly where the drawings say — which is precisely why the drawings have to be yours.

Implementation is where you would expect the story to be a simple victory lap, and instead it hands you a puzzle. Two numbers, and both are true at once. Industry surveys report productivity improvements of twenty-five to thirty-nine percent when developers work with AI. And a careful study by a research group called METR found that experienced developers using AI assistants actually took nineteen percent longer on certain tasks. Not because the AI wrote bad code slowly — because it wrote plausible code quickly, and the time went into verifying, debugging, and correcting that output. Remember the agent's temperament: a brilliant, tireless, slightly over-eager junior teammate that does exactly what you asked, including the parts you did not actually mean. Plausible-but-wrong is its signature failure, and plausible-but-wrong takes longer to catch than obviously broken ever did. Both numbers are honest measurements of the same tool in different hands. Reconciling them is the lesson. AI does not eliminate implementation work so much as transform it — from writing to reviewing, guiding, and verifying. Think of dictating a letter instead of writing one by hand. The words appear faster than your pen could ever move, but you still read every line before you sign, because your name is at the bottom. If you measure yourself only on the speed of the first draft, you will feel like a wizard; the feature materializes in minutes. Measure on shipped-and-correct — the feature in a user's hands, doing the right thing on the third rainy Tuesday of the month — and the discipline still decides. Which of those two numbers describes your week comes down to habits: whether the tests existed before the agent started, whether the specification was clear enough to check against, whether you actually read what came back. Generation is cheap now. Correctness costs roughly what it always cost. The developers who come out ahead are the ones who pay that cost efficiently, up front, with automated checks — instead of expensively, late, through a bug report.

Which brings us to testing, and to a quiet inversion. The obvious transformation first: AI changes test generation itself. Agents can produce test cases, including edge cases and property-based tests, that humans might not think of. A property-based test, if the term is new, works like this: instead of saying, given the number three, expect nine, you state a rule that must hold for whole families of inputs — sorting a list twice must give the same result as sorting it once — and the machinery generates hundreds of inputs and hurls them at your rule, hunting for the one that breaks it. Humans rarely write these, because inventing hostile inputs by hand is tedious. Machines are gleefully tireless at it. But the bigger idea is what tests become in this new life cycle: tests and evals become the primary mechanism for communicating intent to AI agents. A well-written suite tells the AI what correct means, and provides an automated way to verify it. Your test suite used to be a safety net stretched behind you. Now it is also a language you speak forward — the most precise specification you will ever hand the machine, because it is a specification the machine can run.

The paper wires these practices into what it calls a quality flywheel, and the shape is worth carrying around loosely: evaluate against a benchmark suite; diagnose failures by clustering their root causes; fix the prompts or tools that caused them; verify the fixes against a regression suite; and monitor for new failure modes. Then go around again. Each cycle compounds — the wheel spins a little easier every turn, because every failure you catch becomes a check that catches it forever after.

Echo runs a small-scale version of that flywheel, and two habits show its temperament. First: every schema migration — every change to the shape of the database — must carry a test proving the schema really changed. The test builds a database from nothing, runs every migration in order from the very first one, and then checks that the new column or table actually exists. That sounds paranoid, right up until the day a migration silently does nothing and the test is the only witness.
